question: Define a subgraph and explain the difference between a spanning subgraph and an induced subgraph.
expectedPoints: A subgraph H of a graph G is a graph where V(H) ⊆ V(G) and E(H) ⊆ E(G), and every edge in E(H) has both endpoints in V(H).
A spanning subgraph is a subgraph that contains all the vertices of the original graph G (V(H) = V(G)).
An induced subgraph is a subgraph formed by selecting a subset of vertices S ⊆ V(G) and including every edge from G that has both endpoints in S.

question: Given a graph G, if you remove a single vertex v from G to form a subgraph H, what happens to the edges incident to v? Is H an induced subgraph? Explain.
expectedPoints: When a vertex v is removed, all edges incident to (connected to) v are also removed from the subgraph H.
Yes, the resulting subgraph H is an induced subgraph.
It is induced by the vertex set V(G) \ {v} because it contains all edges from G that have both endpoints in this new set.

question: Compare and contrast the concepts of a subgraph, a spanning subgraph, and a spanning tree. Use a small example graph to illustrate the hierarchical relationship between these three structures.
expectedPoints: All spanning trees are spanning subgraphs, and all spanning subgraphs are subgraphs. This describes a hierarchy of containment.
A subgraph only requires a subset of vertices and a subset of edges (respecting endpoints).
A spanning subgraph is a special type of subgraph that includes every vertex of the original graph.
A spanning tree is a special type of spanning subgraph that is a tree (connected and acyclic) and connects all vertices.
